personality_voice.book_baseline: Lucius is defined by cold aristocratic contempt, calculated cruelty, and a veneer of respectability masking genuine menace. He speaks in measured, cutting sentences - never shouting, always implying. He uses social superiority as a weapon ('Dear me, what's the use of being a disgrace to the name of wizard if they don't even pay you well for it'). He is strategically self-controlled: he restrains Draco's complaints with a 'quelling look', warns him not to appear 'less than fond of Harry Potter' for political reasons, and corrects Draco's grades coldly. His cruelty is precise and deliberate. In Deathly Hallows, this composure collapses entirely - he is 'yellowish and waxy', his hand shakes, he whispers, he is desperate and broken. The arc from imperious to humiliated is a defining personality trajectory.
personality_voice.film_portrayal: The films preserve the cold aristocratic register well in CoS and GoF. Film Lucius speaks with the same measured contempt ('Don't boast, Draco. There's no need with these people'), uses the same social weaponry against the Weasleys, and delivers the Borgin and Burkes scene with near-identical dialogue and tone. In OotP he is suave and threatening in the DoM. In DH Part 1, the broken, sallow Lucius is visually and verbally rendered faithfully - he is passive, trembling, and desperate. The film adds one notable deviation: in the Malfoy Manor scene, film Lucius is visibly excited and almost frantic ('Look close, Draco... everything will be forgiven'), which partially matches the book but the book renders this with more controlled desperation whereas the film makes him slightly more agitated and wine-glass-clutching, softening the cold precision of his earlier persona into something more pathetic earlier than the books do.
personality_voice.difference: The main loss is the book's subtler register of Lucius's political intelligence and self-awareness. The Borgin and Burkes scene loses his explicit political calculation ('it is not prudent to appear less than fond of Harry Potter') - a line that reveals his strategic mind and cynical pragmatism. The film Lucius in CoS is slightly more openly contemptuous and less strategically calculating. The Quidditch World Cup scene in GoF is compressed to a brief exchange, losing the scene where he restrains himself from insulting Hermione only because Fudge is watching - a key moment of calculated self-control. His voice in OotP (the corridor encounter with Harry and Arthur, his Ministry influence) is entirely absent from the film corpus.
personality_voice.penalty_logic: The core personality - cold aristocratic contempt, calculated cruelty, political cunning, and eventual humiliation - is preserved across the films. The loss of the 'prudent' line and the Fudge-watching self-restraint moment removes some of the strategic intelligence layer, but the dominant traits remain intact. Minor simplification warrants a score in the 18-22 range.

narrative_role_agency.book_baseline: Lucius serves multiple structural functions: (1) primary antagonist in CoS, actively engineering the Chamber plot and Dumbledore's suspension; (2) political operator throughout OotP, corrupting the Ministry and placing Umbridge; (3) Death Eater leader in the DoM battle; (4) broken instrument of Voldemort's humiliation in HBP/DH. His agency is highest in CoS - he plants the diary, orchestrates the suspension, and is exposed by Harry. In OotP he is the architect of Ministry corruption (Hermione's analysis: 'Lucius Malfoy must have got him too... Never out of the Ministry, is he?'), leads the DoM Death Eaters, and commands the operation. In DH he is reduced to a passive, terrified host.
narrative_role_agency.film_portrayal: CoS preserves his agency well: he plants the diary (shown in the film via Ginny's cauldron), orchestrates the suspension, and is confronted and exposed. The DoM sequence in OotP shows him leading the Death Eaters and demanding the prophecy. DH Part 1 shows him as a broken figure at Malfoy Manor. DH Part 2 shows him calling Draco to Voldemort's side.
narrative_role_agency.difference: The most significant agency loss is in OotP. The entire book arc of Lucius as Ministry corruptor - bribing officials, placing Umbridge, Imperiusing Bode, manipulating Fudge - is absent from the film. The film reduces his OotP role to the DoM confrontation only. His line 'Haven't you always wondered what was the reason for the connection between you and the Dark Lord?' is given to him in the film, which is faithful, but the broader political architecture he represents is invisible. In the DoM film scene, Sirius punches Lucius - a physical confrontation that somewhat diminishes his cold authority. His wand is disarmed by Harry ('Expelliarmus') in the film, which is not in the book corpus for that scene. The book has him roaring commands and organizing the Death Eaters ('Leave Nott, leave him... we'll split into pairs'); the film compresses this.
narrative_role_agency.penalty_logic: The transfer of his Ministry corruption role to off-screen absence is a meaningful agency loss - it removes his function as political architect and reduces him to a field operative. His CoS agency is well-preserved. His DH role as passive/broken is preserved. The OotP political function is the main gap. Score in the 14-17 range.

motivations_internal_conflict.book_baseline: Lucius's motivations are layered: (1) pure-blood supremacy as genuine ideology; (2) self-preservation and social status - he hedges his bets, distances himself from Voldemort when convenient, and returns when safe; (3) family loyalty, particularly to Draco, which becomes his dominant motivation in DH when Voldemort uses Draco as punishment for Lucius's failures; (4) political ambition - he cultivates Ministry connections for influence. His internal conflict is most acute in DH: he is terrified of Voldemort, desperate for Draco's safety, and stripped of all the power that defined him. The book makes explicit that Draco's mission is 'vengeance for Lucius's mistake' and that Lucius is 'secretly glad to be safe in Azkaban'.
motivations_internal_conflict.film_portrayal: The films preserve the pure-blood ideology and self-preservation motivations clearly. The family motivation is present in DH - his desperation about Draco ('everything will be forgiven') and his asking Voldemort to call off the battle to find his son are both in the film. The political ambition/Ministry corruption motivation is largely absent from the films as a visible driver.
motivations_internal_conflict.difference: The film does not show the internal conflict around Voldemort's return - the book's nuance that Lucius never truly sought Voldemort, ran from the Dark Mark at the World Cup, and was punished for it is present in the book corpus (GoF graveyard scene) but the film's graveyard scene only shows Voldemort pulling off Lucius's mask and Lucius's defensive claim ('I have never renounced the old ways'). The book's explicit framing of Lucius as someone who hedged and was caught out is present but compressed. The motivation of family love as the thing that ultimately supersedes ideology is present in both.
motivations_internal_conflict.penalty_logic: Core motivations (ideology, self-preservation, family) are present. The political ambition motivation is underexplained. The internal conflict around Voldemort loyalty vs. self-interest is present but compressed. Simplified but still correct - score in the 11-13 range.

character_arc.book_baseline: Lucius's arc spans from powerful, untouchable aristocrat (CoS) to politically influential operator (GoF, OotP) to humiliated, imprisoned failure (HBP) to broken, terrified shell (DH). The key turning points are: (1) exposure and sacking as governor in CoS; (2) public identification as Death Eater at the graveyard in GoF; (3) arrest at the DoM in OotP; (4) imprisonment in Azkaban and Draco's mission as punishment in HBP; (5) complete degradation at Malfoy Manor in DH, culminating in running through the battle 'not even attempting to fight, screaming for their son.' The arc ends not with redemption but with survival through desperation and the abandonment of ideology in favor of family.
character_arc.film_portrayal: The films show the broad shape of this arc: powerful in CoS, present at the graveyard in GoF, leading Death Eaters in OotP, broken at Malfoy Manor in DH1, and calling Draco to Voldemort's side (then Draco crossing back) in DH2. The final image of Lucius in DH2 is him calling for Draco - consistent with the book's ending.
character_arc.difference: The arc's middle section is significantly compressed. The HBP arc - Lucius in Azkaban, Draco's mission as explicit punishment for Lucius's failure, Narcissa's desperation - is entirely absent from the film corpus (HBP film scenes with Lucius are not in the film corpus provided). The OotP arrest and its consequences are shown but the aftermath (imprisonment, degradation) is only implied. The arc's turning points are present but several key steps are skipped or off-screen, meaning the audience sees the endpoints without the full journey. The book's explicit statement that Draco's mission is 'vengeance for Lucius's mistake' - the causal link between Lucius's failure and Draco's endangerment - is absent from the films.
character_arc.penalty_logic: The arc exists and its broad shape is preserved, but important steps (the HBP imprisonment arc, the explicit causal link between Lucius's failure and Draco's punishment) are missing. This is an incomplete arc - score in the 8-10 range.

key_relationships.book_baseline: Three key relationships: (1) Lucius/Draco - cold, demanding, contemptuous of weakness, but ultimately the relationship that breaks Lucius; he is proud of Draco's pure-blood status but dismissive of his failures ('I would have thought you'd be ashamed that a girl of no wizard family beat you in every exam'). In DH, his entire motivation collapses into desperate paternal fear. (2) Lucius/Voldemort - a relationship of performed loyalty masking self-interest, which Voldemort sees through and punishes. The power dynamic is one of master/terrified servant by DH. (3) Lucius/Arthur Weasley - genuine mutual contempt and class antagonism, with Arthur representing everything Lucius despises (Muggle-loving, poor, principled).
key_relationships.film_portrayal: The Lucius/Draco dynamic is present in CoS (the Borgin scene, the Flourish and Blotts confrontation) and DH (the Manor scenes). The Lucius/Voldemort dynamic is shown in GoF, DH1, and DH2. The Lucius/Arthur dynamic is shown in CoS.
key_relationships.difference: The Lucius/Draco relationship loses significant texture. The book's Borgin scene shows Lucius being genuinely cold and cutting toward Draco ('that may indeed be all he is fit for'), establishing a demanding, contemptuous parental dynamic that makes the DH paternal desperation more powerful by contrast. The film Borgin scene preserves some of this but softens it slightly. The HBP dynamic - where Draco blames Lucius for his situation and Lucius is absent/imprisoned - is entirely absent. The Lucius/Arthur relationship is well-preserved in CoS but absent thereafter (the book has multiple subsequent encounters). The Lucius/Narcissa relationship, which is important in HBP (her desperation about Draco, her quiet support of Lucius in DH), is present in DH1 but the HBP context is missing.
key_relationships.penalty_logic: The key relationships are mostly faithful in the scenes that exist, but the HBP absence removes important development of the Lucius/Draco dynamic. The Lucius/Arthur relationship is truncated after CoS. Score in the 7-8 range.

complexity_nuance_lost_material.book_baseline: Lucius's complexity comes from several sources: (1) the gap between his public respectability and private menace; (2) his political intelligence - he operates through bribery, intimidation, and Ministry manipulation, not just overt Dark Arts; (3) his self-awareness about his own positioning ('it is not prudent to appear less than fond of Harry Potter'); (4) the contradiction between his ideology and his self-preservation instinct - he ran from the Dark Mark, he hedged his bets, he was punished for it; (5) the Horcrux diary subplot - he used Voldemort's own weapon for personal gain (to discredit Arthur and dispose of incriminating evidence), which Voldemort later punishes him for; (6) his complete degradation in DH, which is more powerful because of the height from which he falls.
complexity_nuance_lost_material.film_portrayal: The films preserve the public/private gap and the overt contempt. The DH degradation is well-rendered. The Borgin scene preserves his political anxiety about Ministry raids. The graveyard scene preserves his defensive self-justification to Voldemort.
complexity_nuance_lost_material.difference: The most significant lost nuance is the political operator dimension - his Ministry corruption in OotP (bribing officials, Imperiusing Bode, manipulating Fudge) is entirely absent from the films, reducing him from a systemic threat to a field-level antagonist. The self-aware political calculation ('not prudent to appear less than fond of Harry Potter') is cut from the film's Borgin scene. The Horcrux diary subplot's full complexity - that Lucius acted unilaterally with Voldemort's property for personal gain, and was punished for it - is present in the book (Dumbledore's HBP explanation) but absent from the films. The book's Snape/Narcissa conversation in HBP, which contextualizes Lucius's failure and its consequences for Draco, is not in the film corpus. The book's final image of Lucius 'running through the crowd, not even attempting to fight, screaming for their son' is a powerful culmination of his arc that the film approximates but does not fully render.
complexity_nuance_lost_material.penalty_logic: The political operator complexity is substantially lost. The self-aware strategic intelligence is reduced. The full causal chain of his failures and their consequences is compressed. The character remains recognizable but the material that made him genuinely threatening as a systemic force - rather than just a contemptuous aristocrat - is largely absent. Score in the 8-10 range.

lost_or_transferred_material: Lucius's explicit political calculation in Borgin and Burkes: 'it is not prudent to appear less than fond of Harry Potter' - cut from film, removing his strategic self-awareness
Lucius restraining himself from insulting Hermione only because Fudge is watching at the Quidditch World Cup - key moment of calculated self-control absent from film
Entire OotP arc of Lucius as Ministry corruptor: bribing officials, Imperiusing Bode, manipulating Fudge, being 'never out of the Ministry'
The causal link between Lucius's diary gambit (acting unilaterally with Voldemort's Horcrux for personal gain) and Voldemort's later punishment of him via Draco's mission
Dumbledore's HBP explanation of Lucius's diary scheme and Voldemort's fury at him for it
Lucius in Azkaban during HBP and Narcissa's desperation about Draco as punishment for Lucius's failure
Draco blaming Lucius for his situation in HBP ('much less since his father has lost favor')
The Buckbeak/Hippogriff case in PoA - Lucius operating through legal/political channels to intimidate the Committee
Multiple post-CoS Lucius/Arthur Weasley encounters establishing ongoing class antagonism
Book's graveyard scene: Voldemort's specific rebuke of Lucius for running from the Dark Mark at the World Cup, establishing the pattern of Lucius's self-interested disloyalty
score_caps_applied: Cap 3 (score above 75 if core book function transferred to another character): Lucius's OotP function as Ministry corruptor is not transferred to another character but is simply absent - this functions as an agency/complexity loss rather than a transfer, but the systemic political threat he represents is effectively removed. Score kept below 75.
Cap 1 (score above 85 if films remove major complexity): Applied - the political operator dimension is substantially removed.
comparative.key_observations: Lucius Malfoy is one of the better-preserved antagonists in the film series, with his core personality, voice, and visual presence faithfully rendered. The films capture his cold aristocratic contempt, his relationship with Draco, his dynamic with Voldemort, and his eventual degradation. However, the adaptation loses a crucial dimension: Lucius as a systemic political threat who operates through Ministry corruption, bribery, and legal manipulation. In the books he is genuinely dangerous because he corrupts institutions; in the films he is dangerous because he is a Death Eater. This reduction from political architect to field operative removes the most interesting layer of his characterization. The HBP arc - his imprisonment, Draco's mission as punishment for his failure, and the explicit causal chain of his downfall - is almost entirely absent from the films, which weakens the dramatic logic of his DH degradation. The score of 71 reflects a character who is superficially faithful and recognizable but has lost the political and strategic complexity that made him genuinely threatening in the books.
